query: low concentration of ionized calcium in the serum

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Parathyroid -- Related Testing -- Ionized Serum Calcium
retrieved_docs.content: Ionized calcium, or free calcium, is the biologically active form of calcium necessary for many functions, including cell signaling, neurotransmission, and muscle contraction. Normal levels range from 4.64 to 5.28 mg/dL. Blood pH can affect ionized calcium levels by changing the binding affinity of albumin for positively charged anions. At low pH, an abundance of hydrogen ions effectively competes with free calcium for binding sites on negatively charged albumin, thus increasing free calcium in the serum.

retrieved_docs.title: Calcium -- Testing Procedures
retrieved_docs.content: Although the range of Na+ and Cl+ concentrations usually observed in serum or plasma does not cause a clinically significant error in measuring free calcium, significant errors can occur with other specimens unless the matrices and the ionic strength of the calibrators and samples are matched closely. Modern electrodes have high selectivity for calcium over Na+, K+, Mg2+, H+, and Li+ ions. [39]

retrieved_docs.title: Electrolytes -- Normal and Critical Findings -- Serum Calcium
retrieved_docs.content: Normal range: 8.8 to 10.7 mg/dL Hypocalcemia: less than 8.8 mg/dL Mild to moderate hypercalcemia: greater than 10.7 10 11.5 mg/dL Severe hypercalcemia: greater than 11.5 mg/dL

retrieved_docs.title: Calcium -- Specimen Requirements and Procedure
retrieved_docs.content: A venous blood sample is required to evaluate serum levels. For children, a lancet needle can be used on a finger, and the sample can be collected in a pipette, slide, or test strip. Analyze whole blood specimens within 15 to 30 minutes of collection. If this is not possible, keep the specimen on ice. On the ice, the specimen is stable for at least 2 hours; however, if concurrent potassium testing is requested on the same specimen, the low temperature leads to a spurious increase in potassium within 1 hour of collection. [29] If the specimen cannot be analyzed within 1 hour, the preferred specimen is serum. Centrifuge the specimen and remove the serum or plasma from the cells within 2 hours of collection. [30]

retrieved_docs.title: Hypocalcemia -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Serum calcium concentration is maintained within a very narrow range. Approximately 45% of the body's calcium is bound to plasma proteins, primarily albumin. Approximately 15% is bound to small anions such as phosphate and citrate. And approximately 40% is in the free or ionized state, which is the active state. Most laboratories report total serum calcium concentration, which ranges between 8.5 to 10.5 mg/dL (2.12 to 2.62 mmol/L). Ionized calcium can also be measured, and the normal range is 4.65 to 5.25 mg/dL (1.16 to 1.31 mmol/L). Numbers below this range are considered to be hypocalcemic. Because the majority of body calcium is bound to albumin, total calcium should always be corrected for albumin level before the diagnosis of hypocalcemia is made. There is an approximately 0.8 mg/dL (0.25 mmol/L) drop in serum total calcium concentration for every 1 g/dL (10 g/L) reduction in the serum albumin concentration.

retrieved_docs.title: Hypercalcemia -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Calcium is the most abundant cation in the human body and plays an integral role in neural transmission, enzyme activity, myocardial function, coagulation, and other cellular functions. Most of the calcium is found in the bones as calcium phosphate, whereas a small percentage is found in cells and extracellular fluids. In the serum, about 45% of calcium is bound to proteins, 45% exists as the active form of free or ionized calcium, and 10% is bound to anions. Normal calcium levels range from approximately 8.9 to 10.1 mg/dL, but this number can vary depending on the laboratory. Serum calcium levels fluctuate based on serum albumin levels, as a large percentage of calcium is bound to albumin. Therefore, calcium levels must be adjusted based on albumin levels. Hydrogen ions also bind to circulating albumin, so both acidosis and alkalosis can affect serum calcium levels. For example, increased hydrogen ions in acidosis take up additional binding sites on albumin, leading to increased free calcium levels. Thus, calcium levels should also be adjusted for serum pH. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Calcium -- Etiology and Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: About 50% of the calcium present in circulation is free (also known as ionized calcium); 40% of serum calcium is bound to proteins, especially albumin (80%) and, secondary, to globulins (20%); and about 10% exists as various small diffusible inorganic and organic anions (eg, bicarbonate, lactate, citrate). The free calcium fraction is biologically active. [6] Because calcium binds to negatively charged sites on proteins, its binding is pH-dependent. Alkalosis leads to an increase in the negative charge of proteins, increasing binding and decreasing free calcium; conversely, acidosis leads to a decrease in negative charge, decreasing binding and increasing free calcium. [7] In vitro, for each 0.1-unit change in pH, approximately 0.2 mg/dL (0.05 mmol/L) of inverse change occurs in the serum-free calcium concentration. [8] Calcium can be redistributed among the 3 plasma pools, acutely or chronically, by alterations in the concentrations of protein and small anions, changes in pH, or changes in the quantities of free calcium and total calcium in the plasma. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Parathyroid -- Related Testing -- Total Serum Calcium
retrieved_docs.content: Total serum calcium is the amount of calcium in the blood, including free or ionized calcium, complexed calcium (calcium bound to bicarbonate or citrate), and protein-bound calcium. Normal levels range from 8.5 to 10.2 mg/dL. This value is affected by the amount of protein, usually albumin, available for binding and must be corrected in hypoalbuminemia. For every 1 gm decrease in serum albumin, we expect the total serum calcium to decrease by approximately 0.8 mg. The official equation is: Corrected Calcium = [Ca + 0.02 x (40 - Albumin)]

retrieved_docs.title: Resistant Hypercalcemia -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Hypercalcemia is a serum calcium (Ca) level over two standard deviations above the average mean values. That equates to a total serum calcium level greater than 10.5 mg/dl or an ionized calcium level greater than 5.3 mg/dl. [1] Normal serum calcium levels often range from 9.0 to 10.5 mg/dl. However, values may vary across laboratories. Only about 45% of serum calcium is in the free or ionized state; almost half of the serum concentration is bound to albumin, and about 10% is partially bound to small ions, including phosphate and citrate. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Calcium -- Results, Reporting, and Critical Findings -- Hypocalcemia
retrieved_docs.content: Treatment for hypocalcemia depends on the severity of the symptoms. Patients experiencing mild symptoms should be treated with calcitriol 0.5 mcg orally twice daily and calcium in the combined form of calcium carbonate with the dose of 500 mg orally 4 times daily to achieve optimal absorption. There are several ways to administer calcium. Calcium carbonate can be taken orally via pill or crushed and mixed with liquid. The absorption of calcium carbonate is dependent on stomach acidity. For better absorption, this may be taken with food or substituted with calcium citrate to stimulate an acidic environment. [63] If serum calcium levels are persistently low, possible hypomagnesemia must be evaluated and corrected to normalize serum calcium levels. [56]

retrieved_docs.title: Calcium -- Interfering Factors
retrieved_docs.content: Certain medications may precipitate inaccurate measurements of calcium levels. Perchlorate has been reported to cause discrepancies in ionized calcium measurements, leading to false diagnoses of hypo or hypercalcemia. [42] Medications known to increase serum calcium levels are thiazide diuretics, lithium, antacids, and vitamin D supplements. [43]

retrieved_docs.title: Resistant Hypercalcemia -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Any state of dehydration or the process of hemoconcentration that occurs during venipuncture can falsely elevate total serum albumin levels and falsely account for elevated total serum calcium levels, whereas ionized calcium may remain normal. Changes in blood pressure can also alter serum albumin–calcium-binding. That is, alkalosis enhances binding, whereas acidosis decreases binding. Also, factors that cause low serum albumin levels, such as malnutrition and liver cirrhosis, will lead to falsely low reported serum calcium levels. Therefore, calcium levels must be corrected for abnormal serum albumin levels before the diagnosis of hypercalcemia can be established. This can be calculated as the measured total serum calcium (Mg/dL) + [4.0 - serum albumin (g/dL) X 0.8] or measured total serum + [40 - serum albumin (g/L) X 0.02]. [3] Hypercalcemia can be grouped based on serum calcium levels as follows:

retrieved_docs.title: Primary Hyperparathyroidism -- Pathophysiology -- Normal Calcium Homeostasis
retrieved_docs.content: Caution is necessary when evaluating normal total serum calcium levels in patients with hypoalbuminemia. Such patients may have elevated ionized calcium levels and are truly hypercalcemic. Conversely, the ionized calcium is often normal when there is a low total calcium concentration in the presence of hypoalbuminemia.

retrieved_docs.title: Electrolytes -- Normal and Critical Findings -- Serum Magnesium
retrieved_docs.content: Normal range: 1.46 to 2.68 mg/dL Hypomagnesemia: less than 1.46 mg/dL Hypermagenesemia: greater than 2.68 mg/dL

retrieved_docs.title: Calcium -- Testing Procedures
retrieved_docs.content: Calcium ISEs contain a calcium-selective membrane that encloses an inner reference solution of calcium chloride, often containing saturated silver chloride (AgCl) and physiologic concentrations of sodium chloride and potassium chloride, and an internal reference electrode. The reference electrode, usually Ag/AgCl, is immersed in this inner reference solution. Modern calcium ISEs use liquid membranes containing the ion-selective calcium sensor dissolved in an organic liquid trapped in a polymeric matrix. [38] Because ISEs measure ion activity, they are affected by the ionic strength of a specimen. Free calcium analyzers (and the associated calibrators) are optimized for serum, plasma, or whole blood specimens. Because the ionic strength of these fluids is primarily a result of the concentrations of Na+ and Cl− ions, calibrators are usually prepared in buffer and sodium chloride with a final ionic strength of 160 mmol/kg. [36]

retrieved_docs.title: Milk-Alkali Syndrome -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Elevated serum calcium levels are usually an incidental discovery. Arterial blood gas analysis reveals metabolic alkalosis. Serum albumin levels should be checked in all patients with hypercalcemia, and corrected calcium levels must be calculated in patients with hypoalbuminemia. Ionized calcium levels can also be considered to avoid confounding values from hypoalbuminemia. With hypercalcemia, the next step in diagnosis is measuring serum parathyroid hormone levels, which are low in patients with the milk-alkali syndrome. [19] [20] This finding excludes primary hyperparathyroidism and familial hypocalciuric hypercalcemia during diagnostic evaluation. Serum phosphorus levels are low due to low parathyroid hormone levels but have been reported to be high in the classic syndrome due to high quantities of milk intake. Serum magnesium levels are usually low as hypercalcemia inhibits its reabsorption in the renal tubules; however, renal failure can cause hypermagnesemia due to the inability to secrete magnesium, so this should be considered. [19] Blood urea nitrogen and creatinine can indicate the severity of renal damage.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2449
retrieved_docs.content: Sodium phosphate is classified among the drugs used for treatment of hypercalcemia, indicating its role in managing elevated calcium levels.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1435
retrieved_docs.content: Parathormon, also known as parathyroid hormone, is classified as a member of the “parathyroid hormones and analogues” group. It is employed therapeutically to correct نقص كالسيوم الدم (blood calcium deficiency or hypocalcemia), reflecting its central role in raising serum calcium levels. Calcitonin is described as having a relationship with parathormon, highlighting the complementary and often opposing actions of these two hormones in the regulation of calcium metabolism. Understanding the classification of parathormon, its use in treating hypocalcemia, and its interaction with calcitonin is essential for managing disorders of calcium balance and for guiding appropriate patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 1903
retrieved_docs.content: Parathormon, also known as parathyroid hormone, is classified within the functional group “hormones by function.” It is employed therapeutically to treat نقص كالسيوم الدم (hypocalcemia, a deficiency of calcium in the blood). Parathormon has a physiological relationship with kalcitonin (calcitonin); the two hormones interact to regulate calcium homeostasis, often exerting opposing effects to maintain appropriate serum calcium levels. Understanding the role of parathormon in calcium deficiency treatment and its interplay with kalcitonin is essential for effective management of disorders of calcium metabolism.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1916
retrieved_docs.content: Calcium acetate and calcium phosphate dibasic are both classified as calcium compounds. As members of the broader calcium compounds category, they share the fundamental property of containing calcium ions, yet they are applied differently in medical practice. Calcium acetate is frequently used as a phosphate binder for patients with chronic kidney disease, helping to control serum phosphate levels, while calcium phosphate dibasic serves as a calcium source in nutritional supplements and supports bone health. Recognizing that both agents belong to the calcium compounds group aids clinicians in understanding their chemical characteristics and therapeutic roles.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 1950
retrieved_docs.content: Calcium compounds constitute a class of chemical substances that contain calcium, and this class includes calcium glycerophosphate, calcium acetate, and calcium oxide. Calcium glycerophosphate is a calcium‑phosphate salt that can serve as a source of both minerals. Calcium acetate is commonly employed as a phosphate‑binding agent in patients with renal failure to reduce serum phosphate levels. Calcium oxide, also known as quicklime, can be used as a calcium supplement or antacid in various medical contexts. All three substances are categorized under the broader term “Calcium Compounds,” reflecting their shared elemental composition while each possesses distinct chemical properties and clinical applications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1926
retrieved_docs.content: Acidosis (الحماض) and hyperkalemia (فرطُ بوتاسيوم الدَّم) are medical conditions that frequently co‑occur. In clinical practice, the presence of an acid‑base disturbance such as metabolic or respiratory acidosis is often associated with elevated serum potassium levels, and conversely, high potassium concentrations can exacerbate or signal underlying acidotic states. Recognizing this interrelationship is important for accurate diagnosis, monitoring, and therapeutic management of patients who present with either condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 3 -- Community 2590
retrieved_docs.content: Acetylcholine chloride 10 MG/ML Ophthalmic Solution (brand name Miochol) contains calcium chloride (chlorid vápenatý) as an inactive ingredient. Calcium chloride is contraindicated in patients who have hypercalcemia (فرط كالسيوم الدم), reflecting the risk of further elevating serum calcium levels. Conversely, calcium chloride can be employed therapeutically to address hypocalcemia (نقص كالسيوم الدم), helping to restore normal calcium concentrations. Understanding these relationships is important for clinicians when prescribing or dispensing the ophthalmic solution, ensuring it is avoided in individuals with elevated calcium levels while recognizing its potential utility in treating low calcium states.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 2353
retrieved_docs.content: Calcium protein preparations and calcium lactate are both classified as mineral supplements, representing specific types of calcium‑containing mineral supplement products used to support nutritional needs.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1498
retrieved_docs.content: Oxandrolone, an anabolic androgenic steroid, is contraindicated in patients with hypercalcemia, a disorder marked by elevated serum calcium levels; administering oxandrolone in the setting of hypercalcemia can worsen calcium metabolism disturbances and increase the risk of related complications, so clinicians should avoid prescribing this medication to individuals who have this electrolyte abnormality.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1702
retrieved_docs.content: Parathormon, also called parathyroid hormone, is categorized under “hormones by function” and belongs to the group “parathyroid hormones and analogues.” A key physiologic effect of parathormon is the stimulation of small‑intestine fluid and electrolyte absorption. Clinically, parathormon may be used to treat نقص كالسيوم الدم (hypocalcemia, or low blood calcium). Parathormon also has a defined relationship with kalcitonin, another hormone involved in calcium regulation, underscoring the coordinated hormonal control of calcium and electrolyte homeostasis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 2164
retrieved_docs.content: Calcium glubionate, calcium lactate, and calcium levulinate are all classified as calcium supplements. Each of these compounds provides elemental calcium and is used to correct calcium deficiency, support bone mineralization, and maintain overall calcium homeostasis in patients who require supplemental calcium intake. By belonging to the same therapeutic class of calcium supplements, they share similar clinical indications despite differences in their chemical forms and bioavailability.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1820
retrieved_docs.content: Electrolyte imbalance (اختلال توازن الإلكتروليتات) is a disorder characterized by abnormal concentrations of key ions in the blood, and it encompasses several specific clinical entities. One component of this disorder is potassium deficiency syndrome (متلازمة نقصُ البوتاسيوم في الدَّم), which reflects low serum potassium levels and can lead to muscle weakness, cardiac arrhythmias, and metabolic disturbances. Another manifestation is an increased anion gap (زيادة الفجوة الأنيونية), indicating the presence of unmeasured anions such as lactate or ketoacids and often signaling metabolic acidosis. Hyperuricemia (فرط حمض يوريك الدم), the elevation of serum uric acid, may also arise within the context of electrolyte disturbances and is associated with gout, kidney stones, and renal impairment. Finally, low blood chloride (نقص كلوريد الدم) represents hypochloremia, a condition that can exacerbate metabolic alkalosis and affect fluid balance. Together, these conditions illustrate the diverse ways in which electrolyte homeostasis can be disrupted, highlighting the importance of comprehensive assessment and correction of electrolyte abnormalities in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 4 -- Community 2776
retrieved_docs.content: Chlorid vápenatý, a calcium chloride preparation, is contraindicated in patients with رَجَفانٌ بطيني (ventricular fibrillation) and in those who have فرط كالسيوم الدم (hyper‑calcemia), because administration of calcium chloride in these conditions can exacerbate cardiac arrhythmia or further increase already elevated serum calcium levels.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 1325
retrieved_docs.content: Parathormon, also known as parathyroid hormone, belongs to the class of parathyroid hormones and analogues and is classified as a hormone by its disposition. One of its key physiologic effects is the reduction of renal calcium excretion, which helps to raise and maintain serum calcium levels. Calcitonin is noted to have a relationship with parathormon, reflecting the complementary and often opposing actions of these two hormones in the regulation of calcium homeostasis. Together, these relationships highlight the central role of parathormon in calcium metabolism and its interaction with other calcium‑regulating hormones.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2020
retrieved_docs.content: Potassium ion and potassium supplements are directly linked, as potassium supplements are formulations designed to provide potassium ion to the body. By delivering potassium ion, these supplements help correct potassium deficiencies, support normal electrolyte balance, and maintain proper cellular function, making them an important therapeutic option in the management of conditions such as hypokalemia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 4 -- Community 2713
retrieved_docs.content: Calcium glubionate and calcium lactate are both classified as mineral supplements, representing specific calcium‑containing compounds used to augment dietary calcium intake. As members of the broader mineral supplements category, these agents provide bioavailable calcium for patients requiring supplementation, supporting bone health and metabolic functions that depend on adequate calcium levels.
